# Architectural Drawing Description

This is a historical architectural blueprint on aged parchment paper featuring detailed construction drawings of a Victorian-era building. The main view shows a longitudinal section (side elevation) of a large residential or institutional structure with multiple stories and distinctive architectural features. The building displays a prominent tall tower or turret on the left side with a pointed spire, multiple peaked roof sections with dormer windows, decorative railings along upper levels, and numerous regularly-spaced windows and doors arranged in rows. The structure shows elaborate detailing including arched windows on lower levels, horizontal banding on the facade, and ornamental balustrades. In the upper right corner of the page is a separate detailed cross-section view labeled "Transverse Section," showing the internal spatial arrangement and roof structure of the building. Various handwritten annotations and measurements appear throughout both drawings in faded ink, including construction details and dimensional notations. A color reference strip appears along the right edge of the document. The entire composition is rendered in brown and sepia-toned ink lines typical of period architectural documentation, with the aged paper showing discoloration and wear consistent with a 19th-century document.

# Architectural Floor Plan Description

This is a historical architectural drawing on aged, cream-colored paper showing a detailed second floor plan. At the top of the image is a color reference scale bar with sections of dark blue, cyan, green, yellow, magenta, pink, white, and dark gray, along with a measurement ruler.

The floor plan itself is drawn in brown ink with brown shading and contains multiple rooms and spaces. The layout shows two primary large chambers in the upper portion labeled "Bedroom" with dimensions noted as "12'6" x 15'6"", connected by a central space. Below these are additional rooms including multiple "Bed Rooms," "Bath Rooms," and a "Hall." Various doorways, closets, and architectural features are indicated with dotted lines and small rectangular symbols.

At the bottom of the plan, text reads "SECOND FLOOR" and indicates "SCALED: EIGHT FEET TO ONE INCH." The architect's information appears in the lower right: "F. MELDRUM & SON, ARCHTS—" followed by what appears to be an address "330 PINE ST." 

The paper shows significant age-related staining, foxing, and discoloration with brown marks and spots scattered throughout, particularly in the upper left and lower right corners, indicative of its historical nature.

# Architectural Floor Plan Description

This is a historical architectural blueprint showing the first floor layout of a building designed by E. M. McDougall & Son Architects, located at 330 Pine Street. The document is drawn on aged, tan paper with visible stains and wear marks.

The plan displays a symmetrical layout organized vertically with multiple functional zones. At the top is a residential area containing a "Bed Room" (12' x 16'), a "Kitchen" (13' x 15"), and a "Pantry" with various smaller closets and passages. The central section features two large dining rooms labeled "Old Ladies Dining Room" (30' x 30') on the left and "Children's Dining Room" (30' x 30') on the right, separated by a central hallway. Two parlors flank the dining rooms on either side, each measuring 16' x 16'. Below this middle section are multiple smaller rooms including an "Old Nursery," "Children's Entrance Hall," and various other service areas.

The bottom portion contains the main institutional floor space with a large central "Hall" (approximately 45' x 45') flanked by two identical "Hall Rooms." Additional bedrooms, sitting rooms, and utility areas are distributed around the perimeter. Stairs are indicated throughout the plan at multiple locations.

The drawing includes detailed measurements for all rooms, doorways, and corridors. A color-coded scale bar appears at the top, and the notation "Scale: Eight Feet To One Inch" is indicated at the bottom.
